title: The power to impose conditions — and its legal limits
paras: Let's start with the power to impose conditions — and its legal limits. The power to attach conditions comes from section 70, subsection 1, paragraph A of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990, which permits an authority to grant permission "subject to such conditions as they think fit". Now, that discretion reads open-ended, and it's nothing of the kind. It's bounded by the supplementary provisions of section 72, by the common-law validity tests, and by national policy. Section 72, subsection 1 expressly authorises two things. First, conditions regulating land under the applicant's control beyond the application site, where that's expedient for the development. And second, conditions requiring removal of works, or cessation of a use, after a specified period — temporary permissions. Sections 91 and 92 supply the default time limits: three years to begin a full permission, and the twin limits on outline permissions.

The legal validity tests were settled in Newbury District Council against the Secretary of State, reported at 1981, A-C 578. A valid condition must be imposed for a planning purpose and not an ulterior one. It must fairly and reasonably relate to the permitted development. And it must not be so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have imposed it. A condition failing any limb is ultra vires. The wider proposition — that conditions may serve any purpose within the scope of the planning Acts — traces back to Pyx Granite against the Ministry of Housing, reported at 1960, A-C 260. The Newbury tests predate the policy tests, and they survive them. They overlap, but they're conceptually distinct — one goes to legality, the other to soundness.

Where a condition is void, the question becomes severance. Can the condition be struck out leaving the permission intact, or is it so fundamental that the permission falls with it? The courts distinguish fundamental from incidental conditions, following Kent County Council against Kingsway Investments, reported at 1971, A-C 72. In practice, though, neither an authority wishing to be rid of its own condition nor an applicant challenging one relies on severance. The working route is a section 73 application, which asks the question cleanly — and preserves the permission whatever the answer.

So the structure worth carrying into every conditions problem is three-layered. Statute — what may lawfully be required. Common law — whether this condition is valid at all. And policy — whether it should have been imposed. Most day-to-day arguments are policy arguments dressed as legal ones. Knowing which layer a complaint actually engages determines the remedy — negotiation, section 73, appeal, or judicial review — and the deadline attached to it.

title: The six tests and the policy framework
paras: Now for the six tests and the policy framework. National policy requires conditions to be kept to a minimum, and imposed only where they pass six tests, set out at paragraph 57 of the N-P-P-F — the December 2024 edition. Necessary. Relevant to planning. Relevant to the development to be permitted. Enforceable. Precise. And reasonable in all other respects. The same paragraph directs that conditions requiring discharge before commencement "should be avoided, unless there is a clear justification". And paragraph 56 supplies the anterior principle: conditions may make acceptable what would otherwise be refused, and obligations should be used only where a condition cannot do the job. The operative detail sits in the Planning Practice Guidance, "Use of planning conditions" — reference I-D 21-a.

The tests repay individual attention, because each one fails differently. Necessary means the permission would have to be refused without the condition — so a merely desirable condition, or one restating what the applicant would do anyway, fails. Relevant to planning is the first Newbury limb: no collateral objectives, and no duplicating other regulatory regimes for their own sake. Relevant to the development permitted stops an authority using a house extension to secure works on an unrelated part of the applicant's landholding.

Enforceable asks whether a breach could practically be detected and remedied. Conditions that depend on the cooperation of third parties the applicant cannot compel usually fail here — which is why positive third-party obligations go into a section 106. Precise means the applicant can tell from the wording exactly what is required. Works "to the satisfaction of the local planning authority", with no objective standard, is the classic offender. And precision matters doubly, because conditions are construed against a criminal-enforcement backdrop. Reasonable in all other respects is the residual Wednesbury limb — and the P-P-G is explicit that conditions must not impose unjustifiable and disproportionate financial burdens.

The lineage matters for older permissions. The six tests originated in Circular 11 of 95, which was cancelled in England on the 6th of March 2014, when the P-P-G launched. Superseded, then — though its reasoning is still cited persuasively, and its Appendix A model conditions still circulate. Wales kept the substance, in Welsh Government Circular 016 of 2014 — one of several points where the Welsh regime diverges.

title: Categories of condition
paras: Let's turn to the categories of condition. Conditions are conventionally grouped by when they bite, and the classification drives both the discharge mechanism and the consequences of breach. Pre-commencement conditions — conditions precedent — prohibit any development until a requirement is met. The typical wording runs "No development shall take place until" the requirement "has been submitted to and approved in writing". They're the most consequential class, because breach of a true condition precedent may mean the permission was never lawfully implemented at all. That's the Whitley principle, which has its own topic in this guide.

Conditions requiring subsequent approval of details — materials, landscaping, drainage, construction management — are the everyday subject of discharge applications, and they don't necessarily bar commencement. Whether such a condition also operates as a bar depends on its precise wording, not its subject-matter. The same drainage detail can be an administrative step on one permission, and a trap that voids implementation on another — purely on drafting.

Occupancy and use conditions restrict who may occupy — the agricultural occupancy condition being the classic — or confine use within a class, or remove permitted development rights. P-D-removal conditions are construed strictly and must be precise; a blanket removal without justification is vulnerable. Temporary conditions, authorised by section 72, subsection 1, paragraph B, require cessation or removal after a period. And the P-P-G warns against repeated temporary permissions, and against demolition conditions on permanent structures.

Negative conditions — Grampian conditions — prohibit development until something happens, often off-site, and sometimes on land outside the applicant's control. They're the accepted device for securing what a positive condition could not lawfully require. Grampian Regional Council against Aberdeen, reported at 1984, 47 P and C-R 633, established them. British Railways Board against the Secretary of State, reported at 1994, J-P-L 32, relaxed the old "reasonable prospects" requirement. A Grampian condition isn't objectionable merely because compliance looks unlikely — though a permission may still be refused where there's no reasonable prospect of the action within the life of the permission.

title: What a condition cannot require — and the s100ZA control
paras: Next, what a condition cannot require — and the section 100-Z-A control. A condition cannot require an applicant to give up land or rights. The Supreme Court settled the point in D-B Symmetry Limited against Swindon Borough Council, reported at 2022, U-K-S-C 33. A planning condition cannot lawfully require a landowner to dedicate land as public highway, or to grant rights over its land. That confirmed that Hall and Co against Shoreham-by-Sea U-D-C, reported at 1964, 1 W-L-R 240, remains good law — an authority cannot use a condition to extract, without compensation, what it would otherwise have to acquire. And because D-B Symmetry turned on construction, ambiguous conditions of this kind are read down, so as not to impose an unlawful positive obligation. Which makes the drafting decisive.

Requirements of that character — dedication, transfer, payment, rights — must be secured by agreement: a section 106 obligation, or a section 38 or section 278 agreement under the Highways Act. Otherwise, the choice between condition and obligation is governed by the same necessity logic, plus regulation 122 of the C-I-L Regulations 2010 — necessary; directly related; and fairly and reasonably related in scale and kind. The default, reinforced by paragraph 56 of the N-P-P-F, is that whatever can properly be done by condition should be. Conditions are cheaper, quicker, and need no deed. Obligations earn their place where a condition would fail on enforceability or reasonableness — positive obligations, financial contributions, control beyond the red line.

Since the 1st of October 2018, an authority in England cannot impose a pre-commencement condition without the applicant's written agreement, unless a statutory exception applies. That's section 100-Z-A of the 1990 Act, together with the Pre-commencement Conditions Regulations 2018 — S-I 2018 number 566. Where negotiation fails, the authority serves notice of the proposed condition, with its full text and reasons. The applicant then has a substantive period to respond — a minimum of ten working days. Agreement in writing lets the condition through. A substantive objection blocks it in those terms. And silence lets the authority proceed.

The practical effect is to force pre-commencement substance to the front of the determination, and to hand applicants leverage to convert bar-to-commencement wording into conditions that merely reserve details. But it's a procedural control on imposition only. It doesn't soften the legal effect of a pre-commencement condition once validly imposed, and it doesn't apply to reserved matters on outline permissions in the ordinary sense.

title: Discharge: the article 27 machinery
paras: Now, discharge — the article 27 machinery. Three distinct statutory operations are all loosely called "dealing with conditions", and precision matters here, for fees, timescales and legal effect. Discharge — approval of details — satisfies a condition and leaves the permission untouched. Variation or removal under section 73 produces a fresh permission. Non-material amendment under section 96-A alters the existing permission. This topic takes the first; the seventh topic takes the other two.

There's no free-standing "application to discharge a condition" in the 1990 Act. What the practitioner actually makes is an application for the approval, consent or agreement a condition requires, under article 27 of the D-M-P-O 2015. The decision doesn't amend or remove the condition — it records that the reserved details are approved and the requirement met. Three consequences follow. First, discharge doesn't alter the permission or its time limit — approval of details out of time does not resurrect a lapsed permission. Second, the authority is confined to the matters the condition reserved. It can't reopen the principle of development, or bolt on fresh requirements. And third, a refusal is itself appealable.

The application must identify the permission and the condition, include the particulars and plans needed to deal with the reserved matter, and carry the correct fee — without which it isn't validly made, and time never starts. Determination is eight weeks from the day after receipt of a valid application — sixteen for E-I-A development — extendable only by written agreement. One request may cover several conditions. And partial discharge — by phase, by plot, or by part of a condition — is routine on larger sites.

Fees are charged per request, not per condition. From the 1st of April 2026, that's 89 pounds where the condition sits on a householder permission, and 309 pounds for any other development — including Biodiversity Gain Plan conditions. The per-request rule cuts both ways. Bundling several conditions into one submission pays one fee; drip-feeding them as details mature pays a fee each time. Where the authority fails to determine in time, the fee-refund provisions may bite — but the refund trigger and the determination deadline don't coincide, so track them separately.

And sequence is everything on pre-commencement conditions. The discharge lifts the bar to lawful commencement only if it's obtained before development begins. Approval obtained after works start does not retrospectively cure the breach of a true condition precedent. The Whitley line polices the order of events — not merely the substance.

title: Deemed discharge: the self-help remedy
paras: Deemed discharge — the self-help remedy. Deemed discharge is the statutory answer to authority delay. Where it applies, a condition requiring approval is treated as discharged if the authority fails to determine an article 27 application in time, and the applicant follows the notice procedure. The basis is section 74-A of the 1990 Act — inserted by the Infrastructure Act 2015, in force from the 15th of April 2015 — with the machinery in articles 28 to 30 of, and Schedule 6 to, the D-M-P-O.

The mechanism is sequential. There must first be a valid article 27 application. Then, no earlier than six weeks after the authority received it — or a shorter agreed period — the applicant may serve a deemed discharge notice, identifying the application and the conditions, and confirming that no appeal has been made. The condition is then treated as discharged on the later of two dates: the expiry of the statutory determination period, and fourteen days after the authority receives the notice. If no decision has issued by that date, the applicant may proceed as if the details had been approved as applied for.

Schedule 6 takes the sensitive ground out of the regime. Deemed discharge is unavailable for conditions relating to E-I-A development and habitats sites. For flood risk, contaminated land, and sites of special scientific interest. For highways matters requiring an agreement, and for conditions requiring a section 106 or section 278 agreement. For archaeology. For reserved matters on outline permissions. For development under development orders. And for Crown development. On top of that, the parties can agree in writing to disapply section 74-A entirely.

Practice has drawn its own conclusion: the regime is used sparingly. It's unavailable for exactly the conditions where delay is most common, and an applicant proceeding on self-certified details carries the litigation and reputational risk of being wrong. An authority faced with a notice may contest that the regime applied at all — a Schedule 6 exemption, an invalid underlying application so that time never ran, or a defective notice. And a developer who builds on a deemed discharge later found ineffective stands exactly where one who never discharged stands: in breach — and, if the condition was a true precedent, possibly without an implemented permission.

title: Changing the condition: s73, s96A — and the coming s73B
paras: Changing the condition — section 73, section 96-A, and the coming section 73-B. Where the client wants the condition changed or deleted, rather than satisfied, the route is section 73: an application for permission to develop without complying with a condition previously imposed. The authority may grant subject to different conditions, grant unconditionally, or refuse. Two features are fundamental. A section 73 grant is a new, free-standing permission sitting alongside the original — the developer may implement either, and the original survives unless it lapses. And the authority's consideration is confined to the conditions in question — it cannot revisit the principle — though it applies the development plan and material considerations as they stand at the section 73 decision date, not the original one.

The decisive limit is Finney against the Welsh Ministers, reported at 2019, E-W-C-A Civ 1868: section 73 cannot alter the description of development in the operative part of the permission. Within that constraint, the power is generous. Armstrong, reported at 2023, E-W-H-C 176, Admin, and Fiske, reported at 2024, E-W-C-A Civ 1541, confirm that even substantial changes may be made by varying conditions, provided the description is not contradicted. The interpretive rule comes from Lambeth, reported at 2019, U-K-S-C 33. A section 73 permission is construed as a whole, and conditions the parties failed to carry across will not be implied back in — even where the omission was plainly a drafting slip. A condition left off the section 73 notice is simply gone. Fees from the 1st of April 2026: 89 pounds householder, 608 pounds non-major, and 2,076 pounds major.

Section 96-A is the modest sibling: a non-material amendment to the existing permission. No new permission, a 28-day determination — and it can touch the description, where the change is genuinely non-material. There's no statutory definition of non-material. It's fact and degree, against the specific permission, and the authority may not stray into wider merits. Fees from the 1st of April 2026: 46 pounds householder, 309 pounds otherwise. It's the right tool for drafting slips and minor dimensional changes — and it is habitually overreached. A section 96-A approval of a genuinely material change is challengeable, and no foundation for development.

Section 73-B, inserted by the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, will fill the gap Finney exposed: variation of an existing permission, including its description, where the result is "not substantially different" from what was permitted. As at mid-2026, it is not in force — it awaits commencement regulations, and a fee and procedure framework. Until then, the working rule is unchanged. Description untouched — section 73. Genuinely trivial — section 96-A. Anything else — a fresh application.

title: Disputes: appeals, enforcement and the Whitley principle
paras: Finally, disputes — appeals, enforcement, and the Whitley principle. A refusal, or non-determination, of an article 27 application carries a section 78 appeal — almost always by written representations, and confined to whether the details ought to have been approved. A condition itself may be attacked on a section 78 appeal against the grant, or by section 73 with an appeal if refused. And the choice matters, because of section 79. On a section 78 appeal, the Inspector may deal with the application as if it were made to him in the first instance — so an appeal against one condition puts the whole permission at large. Inspectors rarely disturb an unappealed grant, but the exposure is real where the merits are finely balanced. Section 73 avoids it entirely, because the original permission survives whatever happens. Costs follow unreasonable behaviour — a condition that plainly fails the six tests, or a refusal of details that manifestly comply, are the classic grounds.

On breach, the authority chooses its weapon. The breach of condition notice, under section 187-A, is the fast route: compliance within not less than 28 days, no appeal to the Secretary of State — judicial review is the only challenge — and non-compliance is a criminal offence carrying an unlimited fine. The enforcement notice, under section 172, is slower, but it attracts the section 174 appeal, including ground A — the deemed application. The injunction, under section 187-B, covers the flagrant and urgent. And the tools are not mutually exclusive. Time limits follow the reformed section 171-B: ten years for breaches on or after the 25th of April 2024, the former periods applying transitionally to earlier breaches, and section 191 certificates to regularise immunity. The Enforcement guide, and the immunity checker in Tools, cover that ground.

The gravest condition disputes are about implementation. A permission is implemented by a material operation — section 56 — before expiry. But under F G Whitley and Sons against the Secretary of State for Wales, reported at 1992, 64 P and C-R 296, operations carried out in breach of condition are not "permitted by the permission read together with its conditions", and cannot amount to lawful commencement. If the only works before the deadline were unlawful, the permission was never implemented, and it has lapsed. Everything then turns on whether the breached condition was a "true" condition precedent. Set Hart Aggregates, reported at 2005, E-W-H-C 840, Admin — where timing words without prohibitory language did not void thirty years of quarrying — against Greyfort, reported at 2011, E-W-C-A Civ 908, where the wording "before any work is commenced", followed by "shall be agreed", was a true precedent, and the permission was lost. The distilled test: prohibitory language, going to the heart of the permission — both read through the reasonable reader of Trump and Lambeth.

The exceptions are narrow. Whitley itself excused works where the scheme was approved before the deadline. Hammerton and Prokopp rationalised the escape as cases where enforcement would be irrational, or an abuse of power. Henry Boot against Bassetlaw, reported at 2002, E-W-C-A Civ 983, shut the informal-agreement door: planning is a comprehensive code, legitimate expectation faces a high threshold, and comfort from a case officer is not a discharge. Interpretation disputes across all of this are governed by Trump and Lambeth — objective construction, the permission read as a whole, and implication only with great restraint. The court will not rewrite a bad condition, or imply the restriction the authority forgot.
